original_full_answer: Root diseases in peas are among the most destructive problems affecting pea cultivation, causing significant yield losses and plant mortality. The primary root diseases affecting peas include Root rot (caused by Fusarium oxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani). These soil-borne fungal pathogens attack the root system, compromising the plant's ability to absorb water and nutrients, ultimately leading to stunted growth, wilting, and plant death.   Disease Description and Symptoms: Root diseases in peas typically manifest through a combination of above-ground and below-ground symptoms. Above-ground symptoms include yellowing of lower leaves (chlorosis), stunted plant growth, wilting during hot periods despite adequate soil moisture, and premature plant death. Below-ground examination reveals the most characteristic symptoms: darkened, water-soaked roots that turn brown to black, root decay and rot, reduced root mass, and absence of healthy white root tips. In cases of Fusarium root rot, a reddish-brown discoloration of the vascular tissue extending into the stem base is observed. Seedling damping-off may occur in severe infections, where young plants collapse shortly after emergence. The severity of root diseases increases under conditions of poor drainage, heavy clay soils, continuous pea cropping, and soil temperatures between 15-28Â°C with high moisture levels.   Integrated Disease Management:  - Implement a crop rotation of at least 3-4 years with non-leguminous crops such as cereals, brassicas, or onions to reduce soil-borne pathogen populations.   - Select well-drained fields with good soil structure, avoiding low-lying areas prone to waterlogging.  - Improve soil drainage through proper land preparation, including raised bed cultivation.   - Use certified disease-free seeds from reliable sources and treat seeds before sowing.   - Practice deep summer plowing to expose soil pathogens to solar heat and desiccation.   - Maintain optimal plant spacing to ensure good air circulation and reduce humidity around the root zone.   - Remove and destroy infected plant debris immediately to prevent pathogen spread.   - Avoid excessive irrigation and implement drip or furrow irrigation methods rather than flood irrigation.   - Incorporate well-decomposed organic matter to improve soil health and beneficial microbial populations.   - Adjust sowing dates to avoid periods of high soil moisture and temperature favourable for pathogen development.  - Treat the seed with 15 g of Talc based formulation of Pseudomonas fluorescens per kg seed before sowing.    For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
reason: The answer is relevant but incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence and not fully explaining the management strategies for root diseases.

generated_question: What are the methods to manage borer infestation in sugarcane cultivation?
generated_answer: Borer infestation poses a significant economic threat to sugarcane cultivation, substantially reducing cane yield and sugar recovery. The main borers affecting sugarcane are the early shoot borer (Chilo infuscatellus), top borer (Scirpophaga excerptalis), and internode borer (Chilo sacchariphagus indicus). These pests lead to distinct symptoms such as dead hearts in young plants, bunchy tops, and internal tunneling in mature canes, resulting in considerable crop losses if not addressed.    **Pest Description and Damage Symptoms:**  Early shoot borer damages initial growth stages, with larvae burrowing into the central shoot causing wilting and drying of the central leaf, termed 'dead heart.' The top borer targets later growth stages, producing similar dead heart symptoms and a bunchy top appearance. Internode borers form tunnels within cane internodes, diminishing stem strength and juice quality. Infested pl
original_full_answer: "Borer infestation is one of the most economically damaging pest problems in sugarcane cultivation, significantly reducing both cane yield and sugar recovery. The primary borers affecting sugarcane include the early shoot borer (Chilo infuscatellus), top borer (Scirpophaga excerptalis), and internode borer (Chilo sacchariphagus indicus). These pests cause characteristic symptoms such as dead hearts in young plants, bunchy tops, and internal tunneling in mature canes, leading to substantial crop losses if left unmanaged.    **Pest Description and Damage Symptoms:**  Early shoot borer is particularly destructive during the initial growth stages, with larvae boring into the central shoot causing wilting and drying of the central leaf, known as 'dead heart.' The top borer attacks during the later growth stages, causing similar dead heart symptoms and bunchy top appearance. Internode borers create tunnels within the cane internodes, weakening the stem and reducing juice quality. Infested plants show stunted growth, reduced tillering, and in severe cases, complete crop failure in affected patches.    **Chemical Control Measures:**  For effective management of early shoot borer, soil application of **Chlorpyrifos 20% EC** is highly recommended as a primary control measure. Apply Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at the rate of 2.5 liters per hectare as a soil drench around the base of the plants during the early growth stage (30-45 days after planting). This systemic action provides protection during the vulnerable establishment phase. Alternatively, granular formulations like **Carbofuran 3% CG** at 33 kg per hectare or **Phorate 10% CG** at 10 kg per hectare can be applied in the soil at planting or as side dressing during earthing up operations.    **Safety Precautions and Protective Measures:**  When handling and applying organophosphate insecticides like Chlorpyrifos, farmers must wear complete protective equipment including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, chemical-resistant gloves, protective eyewear, and face masks or respirators. Avoid application during windy conditions and never eat, drink, or smoke during handling. Store chemicals in original containers away from food and animal feed. Observe a Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) of at least 21-28 days before harvesting sugarcane for crushing to ensure chemical residues are within safe limits.    **Biological Control Measures:**  Integrated pest management should include biological control agents such as release of **Trichogramma chilonis** egg parasitoids at 50,000 per hectare during the early whorl stage, repeated at 10-day intervals for three releases. **Cotesia flavipes**, a larval parasitoid, is highly effective against internode borers and should be released at 2,500-5,000 parasitoids per hectare.    **Cultural Control Practices:**  Implement the following cultural practices: remove and destroy dead hearts and infested shoots regularly through detrashing; avoid ratooning heavily infested crops; maintain optimal spacing and avoid dense planting; practice crop rotation with non-host crops; use resistant or tolerant varieties when available; ensure proper irrigation and balanced fertilization to promote vigorous plant growth; and undertake timely earthing up operations which help expose and destroy borer larvae.    **Monitoring and Threshold Levels:**  Regular field monitoring is essential. Economic threshold for intervention is when 5-10% of tillers show dead heart symptoms during the vegetative stage. Early detection through weekly field scouting enables timely intervention and prevents severe infestations.    For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer."
reason: The answer describes the problem but does not provide any methods for managing borer infestation.

original_full_answer: Aphids, commonly known as 'chepa' in Hindi, are one of the most damaging sucking pests affecting wheat crops in Haryana and other wheat-growing regions of India. These small, soft-bodied insects can cause significant yield losses if not managed properly during the critical growth stages of wheat.   Visible Symptoms and Damage: Aphid infestation causes multiple problems in wheat crops. Infested plants show yellowing and curling of leaves, particularly on younger foliage. The pests suck plant sap, causing stunted growth and reduced tillering. Heavily infested plants develop a sticky honeydew coating on leaves, which promotes the growth of black sooty mold fungus, further reducing photosynthetic efficiency. During the grain-filling stage, aphid feeding leads to shriveled, lightweight grains with reduced quality and yield losses of 20-40% in severe cases. The pest also acts as a vector for viral diseases like Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV).  These pests multiply rapidly under favorable conditions, with peak infestation occurring during February-March when temperatures range between 20-25°C.   Control Measures: Cultural Control: Implement several preventive measures to reduce aphid incidence. Avoid early sowing of wheat, as early-sown crops are more susceptible to aphid buildup. Remove alternate host plants and weeds from field borders. Maintain balanced fertilization, avoiding excessive nitrogen, which promotes succulent growth attractive to aphids. Ensure proper irrigation management, as water-stressed plants are more vulnerable. Grow resistant or tolerant wheat varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, or DBW-88 that show reduced susceptibility to aphids.  Biological Control: Encourage natural enemies of aphids, including ladybird beetles (Coccinella septempunctata), lacewings (Chrysoperla carnea), and parasitic wasps (Aphidius species). These beneficial insects can provide long-term suppression of aphid populations. Avoid indiscriminate use of broad-spectrum insecticides that harm natural enemies.  Chemical Control: Give a seed treatment of Imidacloprid 48 % FS @ 0.14 kg per 100 kg of seeds. For immediate and effective control of heavy aphid populations, apply Malathion 50% EC @ 400 ml in 250 lit. of water or Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 20 grams per acre (50 g/hectare), PHI-21 Days in 200 L of water, providing excellent systemic action. Or Quinalphos 25 % EC @ 400 ml per acre in 200-400 litres of water. Apply insecticides when aphid population reaches economic threshold level (10-15 aphids per earhead). For best results, spray during early morning or late evening hours when temperatures are cooler. Ensure thorough coverage of both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Repeat application after 10-15 days if infestation persists. Malathian 50 mg mix it in 250 liters of water and sprinkle it on the crop per acre .  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
reason: The answer describes the problem but does not provide any methods for managing aphids.

generated_question: How can algae growth be managed in rice fields?

While some algae can fix atmospheric nitrogen beneficially, excessive algal blooms form dense mats on the water surface, blocking sunlight penetration to rice seedlings, depleting oxygen levels, and competing for essential nutrients. The water may appear greenish or have a foul smell. Affected rice plants show stunted growth, yellowing of leaves due to reduced photosynthesis, and p
original_full_answer: Algae growth in rice fields is a common problem that can compete with rice plants for nutrients, light, and space, potentially reducing crop yields. Understanding proper management strategies is essential for maintaining healthy paddy cultivation.  Description of the Problem: Algae, particularly blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) and other aquatic algae species, proliferate in flooded rice fields due to the presence of standing water, sunlight, and nutrients. While some algae can fix atmospheric nitrogen beneficially, excessive algal blooms form dense mats on the water surface, blocking sunlight penetration to rice seedlings, depleting oxygen levels, and competing for essential nutrients.  Symptoms and Impact: Farmers can identify algae problems by observing green, slimy layers or mats floating on water surfaces or clinging to rice plants. The water may appear greenish or have a foul smell. Affected rice plants show stunted growth, yellowing of leaves due to reduced photosynthesis, and poor tillering. In severe cases, algae can interfere with water management and field operations.  Severity Assessment: The severity depends on the density and coverage of algae. Light infestations covering less than 25% of the water surface may have minimal impact, while moderate to severe infestations (covering 50% or more) require immediate intervention to prevent significant yield losses.  Management and Control Measures:  For severe infestations, chemical control using copper sulphate (Copper Sulfate Pentahydrate) is effective. About 1.0 kg of copper sulphate per acre is recommended to control algal growth effectively.. The copper ions are toxic to algal cells and provide quick knockdown of algae populations. Application should be done during early morning or late evening to avoid plant stress.   For initial or light infestations, cultural practices are recommended: 1. Water Management: Drain the field periodically to expose algae to air and sunlight, which helps in natural decomposition 2. Manual Removal: Use rakes or nets to physically remove algal mats from the water surface 3. Competitive Cropping: Maintain proper plant density to create canopy shade that limits algal growth 4. Nutrient Management: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which promotes algal blooms  Safety Disclaimer: Strictly follow the recommended dose of 1 kg per acre. Do not exceed this amount as it may cause phytotoxicity to rice plants and environmental harm. Observe the pre-harvest interval of 30 days. Avoid application during windy conditions or immediately before rain. Dispose of empty containers safely and never reuse them for other purposes. Keep emergency contact numbers of local health facilities readily available.  Prevention Strategies: Maintain proper field leveling, ensure good drainage systems, practice crop rotation, and adopt integrated nutrient management to minimize algae recurrence in subsequent seasons.  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
reason: The answer describes the problem but does not provide any methods for managing algae growth.

original_full_answer: Green Leaf Hopper (GLH), also known as green rice leafhopper (Nephotettix virescens), is a significant pest of paddy that causes both direct feeding damage and serves as a vector for viral diseases like tungro. Understanding and managing this pest is crucial for maintaining healthy paddy crops.   Pest Identification and Damage Symptoms: Green Leaf Hoppers are small, wedge-shaped insects, approximately 3-4 mm long, with a characteristic green coloration. They feed by sucking sap from rice leaves, causing yellowing and drying of leaf tips (a condition called 'hopper burn'). Heavy infestations lead to stunted growth, reduced tillering, and significant yield losses. More importantly, GLH transmits Rice Tungro Virus, which causes severe economic damage to paddy crops.   Severity Assessment: Monitor your paddy fields regularly, especially 15-45 days after transplanting. Check the undersides of leaves during early morning or late evening when hoppers are most active. Initial infestations show scattered yellowing, while severe infestations display widespread leaf discoloration and plant stunting.   Control Measures:  The most effective means of GLH management is to use GLH-resistant and tungro-resistant varieties, such as IR 50, IR 54, IR 64, CR 1009, PY 3, Co 46, and White Ponni.  For Severe Infestations: Chemical control is recommended when pest population exceeds ETL level. The economic threshold level (ETL) of brown planthopper is 60 insects per 25 net sweeps, or 5 insects per hill at the vegetative stage, 10 insects per hill at the flowering stage, and 2 insects per hill in tungro-endemic areas. If ETL exceeds, spray or broadcast any one of the following insecticides twice at 15 and 30 days after transplanting per acre: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 40-50 ml per acre, thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 40 g per acre, carbofuran 3% CG @ 10 kg per acre, buprofezin 25% SC @ 320 ml per acre, fipronil 5% SC @ 400- 600 ml per acre, fipronil 0.3% G @ 6.7- 10 kg per acre. Use 200-300 liters of water volume for one acre spray.  Biological control: The most abundant predator is the green mired bug Cyrtorhinus lividipennis, which predate on both eggs and nymphs.   Cultural Practices:  - Remove alternate hosts and weeds from field bunds  - Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which attracts hoppers  - Maintain proper plant spacing for good air circulation  - Use light traps to monitor and reduce adult populations - Intercrop soybean in upland rice to reduce the incidence of leafhoppers in rice.  Trap Methods: - Set up light traps to attract and kill the moths.  - Set up light trap one for at least 1 acre. - Set up bird perches (16-18 /acre) of Insectivorous Bird sat vegetative phase of crop. - Monitor through pheromone traps (@ 4-5/acre for timely control measures. Change the lure at 15-20 days intervals   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
reason: The answer provides information about GLH but does not directly address IPM practices for control.

generated_question: What is the optimal temperature range for red pigment development in tomatoes grown in Punjab?
generated_answer: The red pigment in the fruit will develop only when the temperature is between 150 C-300 C. Above this range of temperature, only the yellow pigment formed. When the temperature exceeds 400 C, no pigment will be formed.
original_full_answer: Tomato is an important vegetable crop in Punjab, cultivated successfully under open field and protected conditions.Climate and Soil Tomato is a warm season crop and requires a relatively long growing season with plenty of sunshine and moderate day temperature of 20–280 C. It is sensitive to frost. Under low temperature, the plant growth is restricted and fruit setting is low. The critical factor in the setting of fruits is the night temperature, the optimum range being 15–200 C.The red pigment in the fruit will develop only when the temperature is between 150 C-300 C. Above this range of temperature, only the yellow pigment formed. When the temperature exceeds 400 C, no pigment will be formed. Tomato can be grown in all types of soils, but the soil should be friable. However, it grows best in light soils ranging from sandy loam to loam. Sowing time :  For winter planting, sowing should be done in October and transplanting in November-December.  economically covered with polythene bags of 35 ×25 cm size and of 100- gauge thickness. For February planting, sow seed in the end of November and protect seedlings from frost in the nursery beds by covering with polythene sheets or sarkanda thatch. However, the yield obtained would be comparatively lower from February planted crop than from November planted crop. To ensure successful growing of healthy seedlings from costly hybrid seeds of tomato, grow nursery of this crop under polyhouse (size 24′×13′×6′) made of UV stabilized low density polyethylene microns (800 guage) thickness. Nursery prepare:- Prepare 1.5 m wide and 20 cm high beds in an area of about two marlas (50 m2) to raise seedlings for an acre. Mix 5 quintals of well rotten farmyard manure with the soil and water the beds at least 10 days before sowing Manuare:- Manures and Fertilizers : Apply 10 tonnes of well rotten farmyard manure and plough it into the soil. Add 25 kg of N (55 kg of Urea) along with 25 kg of  P2 O5 (155 kg of Single Superphosphate) and 25 kg of K2 O (45 kg of Muriate of Potash) per acre in a band at 15 cm on one side of the bed mark and prepare the channels. After removing sarkanda/polythene bag cover by mid February, apply 35 kg of N (75kg of Urea) per acre in the rows, but not touching the base of the vines. Mix it with the soil and earth up. irrigation:- 1. Furrow irrigation: First irrigation should be given immediately after transplanting. Subsequent irrigations may be given after 6-7 days during summer and 10-15 days during winter months. Total number of irrigations required is 14 to 15.  2. Drip irrigation, fertigation and plastic mulching: Drip irrigation, fertigation and silver-black plastic mulching in rainy Varieties:- 1.Punjab Ratta’ are determinate with dense & dark green foliage. The fruits are medium sized, oval, very firm suitable for processing due to deep red colour with average lycopene content of 8 mg/100 g and TSS contents of 5%,yields 220-230 q/acre while the February transplanting gives 170-180 q/acre 2.Punjab Varkha Bahar-4, First picking  is  possible 88 days after transplanting. It is suitable for cultivation in rainy/autumn season. It is resistant to leaf curl virus. Its TSS is 3.8% and  lycopene content is 3.13 mg/100g of fresh weight.  Average yield is 245 q/acre. 3.Punjab Swarna (2018),. First picking is possible 120 days after transplanting. It gives early average yield (harvested till end March) of 166 q/acre and average yield of 1087 q/acre. It is suitable for table purpose.

generated_question: Please suggest a solution for late blight disease in potatoes, which is causing the entire potato crop to get destroyed due to this blight disease in Haryana, Rupnagar district.
generated_answer: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is serious disease in potato if not controlled earlier there may be major yield loses. As per the POP of Haryana recommendation for the control of late blight are as follows,  Symptoms: Infected tubers from cold stores serve as primary source of disease. On emergence the sprouts and leaves get infected. Water-soaked spots appear on margins of leaves which later turn into black patches with whitish fungus growth visible on lower surface in the morning hours. Black patches may extend and kill the foliage in a few days if moist weather prevails. Decaying leaves emit offensive odour.  Favourable conditions: Infected seed tubers grow into healthy plants but under favourable conditions for the disease (10-12 C and RH > 80%) development, the disease infects the stem and lower leaves.  Cultural method: Use selected healthy tubers for planting. Infected/rejected tubers taken out of cold stores should be burried and not left in the open. Follow high ridge cult
original_full_answer: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is serious disease in potato if not controlled earlier there may be major yield loses. As per the POP of Punjab recommendation for the control of late blight are as follows,  Symptoms: Infected tubers from cold stores serve as primary source of disease. On emergence the sprouts and leaves get infected. Water-soaked spots appear on margins of leaves which later turn into black patches with whitish fungus growth visible on lower surface in the morning hours. Black patches may extend and kill the foliage in a few days if moist weather prevails. Decaying leaves emit offensive odour.  Favourable conditions: Infected seed tubers grow into healthy plants but under favourable conditions for the disease (10-12 C and RH > 80%) development, the disease infects the stem and lower leaves.  Cultural method: Use selected healthy tubers for planting. Infected/rejected tubers taken out of cold stores should be burried and not left in the open. Follow high ridge culture to avoid tuber infection. Use short-duration varieties. The model specifies that 7 days moving sum of RH > 85% for at least 90 hr coupled with a 7 day moving sum of temperature between 7.2 and 26.6°C for at least 115 hr would predict appearance of late blight within 10 days of satisfying the conditions. Resisitant varieties like (Processing varieties) Kufri Chipsona-1, Kufri Chipsona-3, Kufri Frysona, Kufri Pushkar.  Chemical control: Spray the crop with Propineb 70% WP, Mancozeb 75% WP, or Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 500–700 g per acre, or Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 750–1000 g per acre, mixed in 250–350 litres of water. The spray should be applied in the first week of November as a preventive measure, before the appearance of disease symptoms.  For further details and information please contact your nearby KVK or agricultural extension officer.
reason: Answer is incomplete and cut off, does not provide full solution for late blight disease.

original_full_answer: Paraquat Dichloride 24% SL is a non-selective contact herbicide used for effective weed management in paddy cultivation. Understanding its proper application is crucial for successful weed control while ensuring crop safety and environmental protection.  Herbicide Characteristics:  Paraquat Dichloride (marketed under various generic names) is a fast-acting, non-selective contact herbicide that controls a broad spectrum of annual grasses and broadleaf weeds. It works by disrupting photosynthesis in green plant tissues, causing rapid desiccation and death of exposed vegetation. Being a contact herbicide, it only affects the plant parts it touches and does not have systemic or residual soil activity. It controls weeds like Echinochloa crusgalli, Cyperus iria, Ageratum conyzoides, Commelina benghalensis, Marsilea quadriofoliata, and Brachiaria mutica in paddy.  Recommended Dosage and Application:  For paddy cultivation, the recommended dose of Paraquat Dichloride 24% SL is 0.5-1.4 liters per acre mixed in 200 liters of water. This dosage is effective for controlling emerged weeds in paddy fields. The herbicide should be applied as a directed spray, carefully avoiding contact with the paddy crop plants to prevent damage.  Timing of Application:  Paraquat dichloride is typically used as a pre-planting herbicide in paddy fields. It can be applied 5-7 days before transplanting or sowing to clear the field of existing weeds. In direct-seeded rice systems, it should be applied before rice emergence. For established crops, it can be used as a directed spray between rows, ensuring the spray does not contact the rice plants. Some farmers also use it for weed control on bunds and field margins.  Application Method:  Use a knapsack sprayer with a flat-fan nozzle for uniform coverage. Mix the required quantity of herbicide thoroughly in clean water. Apply when weeds are young and actively growing (2-4 leaf stage) for maximum effectiveness. Ensure complete coverage of weed foliage. Application should be done during calm weather conditions (morning or evening) to prevent drift onto the crop. Avoid spraying during windy conditions or when rain is expected within 6 hours.  SAFETY PRECAUTIONS (MANDATORY):  Paraquat dichloride is highly toxic and requires extreme caution during handling and application. Farmers must wear full protective equipment, including chemical-resistant gloves, a face shield or goggles, a respirator mask, long-sleeved clothing, long pants, and boots. Never eat, drink, or smoke during application. Keep children, livestock, and unauthorized persons away from the application area. After application, wash hands and exposed skin thoroughly with soap and water. Wash contaminated clothing separately before reuse. Store the herbicide in its original container in a locked, well-ventilated area away from food, feed, and children. Empty containers should be triple-rinsed and punctured before disposal according to local regulations. Never reuse empty containers for any purpose. In case of accidental exposure or poisoning, seek immediate medical attention and show the product label to the doctor.  For further guidance or clarification on herbicide selection, application techniques, or integrated weed management strategies specific to your paddy variety and local conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.
reason: The answer provides general information about Paraquat Dichloride but does not specify the recommended dose for paddy cultivation in Kolkata district.

original_full_answer: Manganese sulphate is an essential micronutrient fertilizer used to correct manganese deficiency in wheat crops.   Understanding Manganese Deficiency in Wheat: Symptoms of manganese deficiency begin to appear during the early growth stages of wheat and during the emergence of ears. Brownish - yellow stripes develop on the leaves, starting from the leaf tip and extending downward ; plant growth is reduced, and ears emerge late and twisted.  Manganese deficiency is increasing in rice wheat systems grown in Indo-gangetic plains having highly permeable coarse textured alkaline soils with low organic matter. The principle of any micronutrient including Mn recommendations for different crops and cropping systems is based on its available status in soil, requirements to meet the crops/cropping system and fixation in soil complex and other losses. Durum wheat genotypes are more susceptible to Mn deficiency than aestivum wheat. Manganese (Mn) can be supplied through the sulphate salt of Mn i.e. MnSO4 (Manganese sulphate) either through soil or foliar.  When symptoms of manganese deficiency appear in standing crops, apply 3 to 4 sprays of a 0.5 percent manganese sulfate solution at 10-15 day intervals . The first spray should be before the first irrigation, and the next 2-3 sprays should be after the first irrigation. Mix one kilogram of manganese sulfate in 200 liters of water and spray per acre.  Wheat performed tremendously with application of Mn in deficient soils. Application of Mn enhances the farmers profit by 10-35% depending upon crop type and crop response to Mn fertilization. Replenishment of Mn improves the fertility and soil health, and also increases the utilization of other macronutrients, reducing leaching losses and thus helpful in saving environment.  For further guidance on micronutrient management, soil testing procedures, or clarification on fertilizer application methods, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer in Jind district.
reason: The answer explains manganese deficiency but does not provide the recommended application rate or method for manganese sulphate in wheat in Tamil Nadu.
